question: [File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, given a buy offer with maxAssets > 0 at any tick where tickToPrice rounds down via divHalfDownUnchecked, repeatedly fill 1-unit partial fills so that each fill adds floor(buyerPrice/WAD) = 0 to consumed[maker][group] (when buyerPrice < WAD), allowing unlimited units to be extracted while consumed never reaches maxAssets, violating the invariant that offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets > 0, tick chosen so tickToPrice(tick) < WAD (any tick < MAX_TICK), settlement fee = 0. Call sequence: (1) maker signs buy offer with maxAssets = N, tick < MAX_TICK; (2) taker calls take(offer, ..., units=1, ...) repeatedly; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] after each call. Invariant tested: consumed[maker][group] <= offer.maxAssets after every take. Scoped impact: offer overfill — taker receives unbounded credit without exhausting maker's maxAssets cap. Proof idea: fuzz tick in [0, MAX_TICK-1] and units=1, assert consumed delta == mulDivDown(units, tickToPrice(tick), WAD) and that sum of consumed deltas across N fills equals total units taken * price / WAD.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP = 1e12 quantization in tickToPrice so that for a buy offer with maxAssets = K, the total units extractable across many 1-unit fills exceeds floor(K * WAD / tickToPrice(tick)) by a measurable amount due to systematic rounding loss in mulDivDown(1, tickToPrice(tick), WAD) = 0 for low-priced ticks, violating the invariant that total economic value extracted must not exceed maxAssets? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = K, tick chosen so tickToPrice(tick) < 1e12 (tick near 0). Call sequence: (1) create offer with maxAssets = K, tick = 1; (2) call take with units = 1 repeatedly until revert; (3) count total units taken. Invariant tested: total_units_taken * tickToPrice(tick) / WAD <= maxAssets. Scoped impact: offer overfill — maker's capital cap is bypassed. Proof idea: unit test with tick=1, maxAssets=1e18, assert take(units=1) succeeds more than maxAssets/tickToPrice(1)*WAD times.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.divHalfDownUnchecked / TickLib.tickToPrice] Can an unprivileged market creator or taker identify two adjacent ticks t and t+1 that map to the same quantized price after the PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP floor in tickToPrice (i.e., tickToPrice(t) == tickToPrice(t+1)), then place a buy offer at tick t+1 (higher tick = higher raw price) but have consumed tracking use the same price as tick t, causing the maker to receive fewer assets per unit than their signed tick implies, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind correct price? Preconditions: find t such that floor(rawPrice(t+1) / 1e12) == floor(rawPrice(t) / 1e12). Call sequence: (1) sign offer at tick t+1; (2) taker calls take; (3) assert buyerAssets == units * tickToPrice(t+1) / WAD. Invariant tested: price used in consumed accounting matches the tick the maker signed. Scoped impact: maker undercharged — taker pays less than the tick price implies. Proof idea: enumerate all ticks, find collisions, assert tickToPrice(t) != tickToPrice(t+1) for all t in [0, MAX_TICK-1].

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker grief a sell-offer maker by exploiting mulDivUp rounding in sellerAssets = units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD) for a sell offer with maxAssets > 0, causing each partial fill to consume ceil(units * sellerPrice / WAD) from the group budget instead of the true value, exhausting the maker's maxAssets cap faster than intended and effectively canceling the offer after fewer units than the maker expected? Preconditions: sell offer (offer.buy = false) with maxAssets = N, tick chosen so sellerPrice is not a multiple of WAD. Call sequence: (1) maker creates sell offer with maxAssets = N; (2) taker calls take with units = 1 repeatedly; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] after each fill. Invariant tested: consumed[maker][group] accurately reflects economic value filled, not an overcounted value. Scoped impact: offer griefed — maker's offer exhausted prematurely, reducing their effective liquidity. Proof idea: fuzz units in [1, 1000], assert sum(mulDivUp(u, sellerPrice, WAD)) > sum(u * sellerPrice / WAD) and offer exhausts before expected units.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.wExp / TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the ~1.4 bps maximum absolute error in wExp's 3-term Taylor approximation to cause tickToPrice to return a price that differs from the true sigmoid price by enough to shift consumed accounting by a measurable amount across many partial fills, allowing the total units extracted to exceed what the maker's maxAssets cap should permit at the true price? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets > 0, tick chosen near the inflection point where wExp error is maximized. Call sequence: (1) compute true price vs wExp-approximated price for target tick; (2) fill offer to maxAssets using approximated price; (3) assert total units taken vs expected at true price. Invariant tested: consumed accounting must reflect the maker's intended economic cap, not an approximation-inflated one. Scoped impact: offer overfill — taker extracts more units than maker's maxAssets should allow at true price. Proof idea: differential test comparing wExp output against a high-precision exp implementation for all ticks, assert |tickToPrice(t) - truePrice(t)| < PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, split a large fill into N small fills of units_i each such that sum(mulDivDown(units_i, buyerPrice, WAD)) < mulDivDown(sum(units_i), buyerPrice, WAD), exploiting the sub-additivity of floor division to consume less of the group budget than a single equivalent fill, allowing more total units to be taken than maxAssets / buyerPrice * WAD? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = M, buyerPrice not a divisor of WAD. Call sequence: (1) compute optimal split of N fills; (2) execute N partial takes; (3) compare total consumed vs single-fill consumed for same total units. Invariant tested: sum of consumed deltas across partial fills equals consumed delta of equivalent single fill. Scoped impact: offer overfill — taker extracts more units by splitting fills. Proof idea: fuzz N and units_i, assert sum(mulDivDown(u_i, p, WAD)) <= mulDivDown(sum(u_i), p, WAD) and find cases where strict inequality holds.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, consumed tracks buyerAssets = mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) while the actual credit received is always exactly units, so that the ratio of credit-per-consumed-asset increases as buyerPrice decreases, allowing a taker to extract disproportionately more credit per unit of maker's maxAssets budget at low-priced ticks? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = M, tick near 0 (low price). Call sequence: (1) create buy offer at tick = 4 (lowest valid at DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4); (2) fill with large units; (3) assert credit received vs buyerAssets consumed. Invariant tested: credit received per consumed asset must not exceed 1/buyerPrice. Scoped impact: offer overfill — maker's maxAssets cap does not correctly bound the credit issued. Proof idea: unit test at tick=4, assert units_taken * tickToPrice(4) / WAD == consumed[maker][group] within 1 wei.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a sell offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that consumed tracks sellerAssets = mulDivUp(units, sellerPrice, WAD) while the taker pays buyerAssets = mulDivUp(units, buyerPrice, WAD) where buyerPrice > sellerPrice, causing the consumed counter to advance by sellerAssets (smaller) while the taker pays buyerAssets (larger), creating a discrepancy between the maker's perceived remaining capacity and the actual economic value transacted? Preconditions: sell offer with maxAssets = M, non-zero settlement fee. Call sequence: (1) create sell offer with maxAssets = M, settlement fee > 0; (2) fill partially; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] == sellerAssets not buyerAssets. Invariant tested: consumed tracking must use the correct asset type (sellerAssets for sell offers). Scoped impact: accounting desync — maker's remaining capacity is overstated by the settlement fee component. Proof idea: assert consumed delta == mulDivUp(units, sellerPrice, WAD) and buyerAssets - sellerAssets == settlement fee collected.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the interaction between PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP quantization and mulDivDown rounding to find a tick t and units u such that mulDivDown(u, tickToPrice(t), WAD) < u * rawPrice(t) / WAD, where rawPrice is the unquantized sigmoid value, causing the consumed counter to advance by less than the true economic value and allowing the offer to be filled beyond the maker's intended maxAssets cap? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets > 0, tick chosen to maximize quantization error. Call sequence: (1) compute rawPrice(t) and tickToPrice(t) for all ticks; (2) find t maximizing rawPrice(t) - tickToPrice(t); (3) fill offer to maxAssets; (4) assert total units taken. Invariant tested: consumed[maker][group] accurately reflects the true economic value extracted. Scoped impact: offer overfill — maker's capital is over-committed due to price quantization. Proof idea: fuzz tick in [0, MAX_TICK], compute max(rawPrice(t) - tickToPrice(t)) and assert it is bounded by PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0 and tick = MAX_TICK (price = 1e18), exploit the fact that buyerAssets = mulDivDown(units, 1e18, WAD) = units exactly, so consumed advances by exactly units, but the maker's maxAssets is denominated in loan tokens while units are credit units, causing a 1:1 mapping that may not match the maker's intent when the market has non-trivial settlement fees? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = M, tick = MAX_TICK, non-zero settlement fee. Call sequence: (1) create buy offer at MAX_TICK with maxAssets = M; (2) fill with units = M; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] == M and buyerAssets == M. Invariant tested: maxAssets cap correctly bounds the loan token outflow from the maker. Scoped impact: offer undercharged — maker pays exactly maxAssets in loan tokens but settlement fee is added on top, so total outflow exceeds maxAssets. Proof idea: assert buyerAssets == units when tick == MAX_TICK and settlement fee == 0, then assert buyerAssets > units when settlement fee > 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the group-level consumed tracking to overfill a maker's aggregate position across multiple offers sharing the same group, by choosing fill sizes that minimize the consumed increment per fill due to mulDivDown rounding, so that the sum of consumed increments across all fills is less than the true aggregate economic value, allowing the group budget to be exceeded? Preconditions: maker has two offers in the same group, both buy offers with maxAssets > 0, same tick. Call sequence: (1) fill offer 1 with units = 1 repeatedly until near maxAssets; (2) fill offer 2 with units = 1 repeatedly; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] <= offer1.maxAssets + offer2.maxAssets. Invariant tested: consumption group budget cannot be exceeded across offers. Scoped impact: offer overfill — group budget exceeded due to rounding. Proof idea: stateful fuzz test with two offers in same group, assert consumed[maker][group] <= max(offer1.maxAssets, offer2.maxAssets) after all fills.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that consumed is updated BEFORE the buyer callback is invoked (line 368 before line 445), so that during the callback the consumed counter already reflects the fill but the position state is also already updated, allowing the callback to call setConsumed to reset the group budget and then reenter take to fill the offer again, effectively replaying the fill? Preconditions: buy offer with maker callback, maxAssets > 0, taker controls the callback contract. Call sequence: (1) taker calls take with buyer callback; (2) callback calls setConsumed(group, 0, maker) — but this requires authorization; (3) callback reenters take. Invariant tested: partial fills must update consumed state before any exploitable reentry point. Scoped impact: offer replay — same offer filled twice within one transaction. Proof idea: implement callback that attempts setConsumed reset and reentrant take, assert second take reverts with ConsumedAssets.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, the consumed increment is buyerAssets = mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) where buyerPrice = tickToPrice(tick) (since for buy offers sellerPrice = offerPrice - fee and buyerPrice = sellerPrice + fee = offerPrice), so that the settlement fee does not affect the consumed tracking, allowing a taker to fill the offer at a time when the settlement fee is high (reducing sellerAssets) while the consumed counter advances at the full offerPrice rate, causing the maker to receive fewer assets per consumed unit than expected? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets > 0, settlement fee changes between fills. Call sequence: (1) fill partially at low settlement fee; (2) settlement fee increases; (3) fill again; (4) assert total sellerAssets received by maker vs consumed. Invariant tested: consumed tracking must reflect the actual assets the maker receives, not the gross buyerAssets. Scoped impact: maker undercharged — maker receives less than maxAssets worth of value while consumed reaches maxAssets. Proof idea: assert sum(sellerAssets) < maxAssets when settlement fee > 0 and offer is fully consumed.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a sell offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the mulDivUp rounding in sellerAssets = mulDivUp(units, sellerPrice, WAD) to cause a 1-unit fill to consume ceil(sellerPrice / WAD) from the group budget, which equals 1 when sellerPrice >= WAD (impossible since price <= 1) or 0 when sellerPrice < WAD, and specifically find units where mulDivUp(units, sellerPrice, WAD) > units * sellerPrice / WAD, causing the offer to be exhausted before the maker's intended maxAssets worth of units are sold? Preconditions: sell offer with maxAssets = M, tick chosen so sellerPrice is not a multiple of WAD. Call sequence: (1) create sell offer with maxAssets = M; (2) fill with units = 1 repeatedly; (3) count total units sold when consumed reaches M. Invariant tested: total units sold when offer exhausted equals floor(M * WAD / sellerPrice). Scoped impact: offer griefed — maker sells fewer units than maxAssets should allow. Proof idea: fuzz sellerPrice in [1, WAD], assert sum(mulDivUp(1, p, WAD) for _ in range(N)) >= N * p / WAD.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the interaction between tick spacing reduction (setMarketTickSpacing) and tickToPrice to fill an offer at a newly unlocked tick that maps to the same quantized price as an existing tick, causing the consumed accounting to use the same price as before the spacing reduction, while the maker's ratifier was only designed to authorize offers at the old spacing multiples, potentially allowing fills at ticks the maker never intended to authorize? Preconditions: market with DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4, tick spacing reduced to 2, maker has offer at tick 4. Call sequence: (1) reduce tick spacing to 2; (2) create offer at tick 2 (newly valid); (3) assert tickToPrice(2) vs tickToPrice(4); (4) fill offer at tick 2. Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind correct tick/price. Scoped impact: offer filled at unintended price — maker's authorization does not cover the new tick. Proof idea: assert tickToPrice(2) != tickToPrice(4) for all valid tick pairs after spacing reduction.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.divHalfDownUnchecked / TickLib.tickToPrice] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the unchecked arithmetic in divHalfDownUnchecked where (x + (d - 1) / 2) could theoretically overflow for pathological inputs, causing tickToPrice to return an incorrect price that underestimates the true price, leading to consumed tracking that advances slower than expected and allows the offer to be overfilled? Preconditions: identify inputs to divHalfDownUnchecked where x + (d-1)/2 overflows uint256. Call sequence: (1) call tickToPrice with tick values that maximize the intermediate computation; (2) assert no overflow occurs; (3) if overflow found, demonstrate consumed undercounting. Invariant tested: tickToPrice must return a value in [0, 1e18] for all valid ticks. Scoped impact: offer overfill — incorrect price causes consumed to advance too slowly. Proof idea: fuzz all ticks in [0, MAX_TICK], assert tickToPrice(t) <= 1e18 and no overflow in intermediate computations.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that the protocol comment states 'It is possible to give units to a fully consumed assets-based buy offer with price < 1' (Midnight.sol line 94), meaning that when consumed[maker][group] == maxAssets, a take with units such that mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) == 0 still succeeds, allowing the taker to receive free credit units without advancing the consumed counter? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = M, tick chosen so tickToPrice(tick) < WAD, consumed[maker][group] == M. Call sequence: (1) fill offer to exactly maxAssets; (2) call take with units = 1 where mulDivDown(1, buyerPrice, WAD) = 0; (3) assert credit received > 0 and consumed unchanged. Invariant tested: a fully consumed offer must not allow any further fills that transfer value. Scoped impact: offer reused — taker receives credit from a fully consumed offer. Proof idea: unit test with tick=4, maxAssets=1, fill to maxAssets, then take(units=1) and assert it succeeds with credit increase.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the asymmetry between buy and sell offer consumed tracking — buy offers track buyerAssets (mulDivDown) while sell offers track sellerAssets (mulDivUp) — to construct a sequence of fills across a shared group where the total consumed is less than the true aggregate economic value, allowing the group budget to be exceeded when mixing buy and sell offers in the same group? Preconditions: maker has one buy offer and one sell offer in the same group (violating the 'same direction' recommendation but not enforced). Call sequence: (1) fill buy offer partially; (2) fill sell offer partially; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] <= max(buy.maxAssets, sell.maxAssets). Invariant tested: group budget cannot be exceeded regardless of offer direction mix. Scoped impact: offer overfill — group budget exceeded due to direction mismatch in consumed tracking. Proof idea: stateful fuzz with mixed-direction offers in same group, assert consumed never exceeds the group cap.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that buyerPrice = tickToPrice(tick) (for buy offers, buyerPrice = offerPrice since sellerPrice = offerPrice - fee and buyerPrice = sellerPrice + fee = offerPrice), so that the consumed counter tracks gross buyer outflow including the settlement fee, while the maker's credit position only increases by units (not buyerAssets), causing a mismatch between the consumed counter and the actual credit issued to the maker? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets > 0, non-zero settlement fee. Call sequence: (1) fill buy offer with units = U; (2) assert consumed delta == mulDivDown(U, tickToPrice(tick), WAD); (3) assert maker credit increase == U. Invariant tested: consumed tracking must correctly bound the maker's credit issuance, not just the gross asset flow. Scoped impact: accounting desync — maker's credit cap is not correctly enforced by maxAssets when settlement fee is non-zero. Proof idea: assert consumed[maker][group] / tickToPrice(tick) * WAD > maker_credit_increase when settlement fee > 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the monotonicity property of tickToPrice (higher tick = higher price) combined with PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP quantization to find a range of ticks [t_low, t_high] where all ticks map to the same quantized price, then fill an offer at t_high (highest price in range) while the consumed counter advances at the same rate as t_low, effectively getting the highest-priced offer at the lowest-priced consumed rate? Preconditions: find tick range where tickToPrice is constant. Call sequence: (1) enumerate ticks to find constant-price ranges; (2) create offer at highest tick in range; (3) fill and assert consumed rate. Invariant tested: consumed tracking must reflect the actual tick price, not a lower quantized price. Scoped impact: offer undercharged — taker benefits from price quantization at maker's expense. Proof idea: assert for all t in [0, MAX_TICK-1]: if tickToPrice(t) == tickToPrice(t+1) then the consumed delta for a fill at t+1 equals the delta at t.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the interaction between the continuous fee accrual (which reduces credit via _updatePosition) and the consumed tracking (which does not account for credit reductions) to fill an offer where the maker's effective credit after fee accrual is less than the units taken, while consumed still advances by buyerAssets, causing the maker to be over-committed relative to their net credit position? Call sequence: (1) create buy offer; (2) wait for continuous fee to accrue; (3) fill offer; (4) assert maker net credit (credit - pendingFee) vs consumed. Invariant tested: consumed tracking must correctly bound the maker's net economic exposure. Scoped impact: accounting desync — maker's maxAssets cap does not account for fee-reduced net credit. [File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, the consumed check (require(newConsumed <= offer.maxAssets)) uses the post-increment value, so that a fill that would push consumed from M-1 to M+K (where K > 0) is rejected, but a fill that pushes consumed from M-1 to exactly M is accepted, and then a subsequent fill with units such that mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) == 0 is also accepted, effectively allowing fills beyond maxAssets? Preconditions: buy offer with maxAssets = M, consumed = M-1, tick chosen so mulDivDown(1, buyerPrice, WAD) = 0. Call sequence: (1) fill to consumed = M-1; (2) fill with units = 1 (consumed += 1 = M, passes check); (3) fill with units = 1 again (consumed += 0 = M, passes check). Invariant tested: no fill should succeed when consumed == maxAssets and units > 0. Scoped impact: offer reused — offer continues to be filled after reaching maxAssets. Proof idea: unit test asserting take reverts when consumed == maxAssets and mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) == 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a sell offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that consumed tracks sellerAssets = mulDivUp(units, sellerPrice, WAD) while the taker's debt increases by exactly units, causing a discrepancy where the maker's remaining capacity (maxAssets - consumed) decreases faster than the taker's debt increases, allowing the taker to create less debt than the maker's maxAssets cap implies? Preconditions: sell offer with maxAssets = M, tick chosen so sellerPrice is not a multiple of WAD. Call sequence: (1) fill sell offer with units = 1 repeatedly; (2) assert total debt created vs consumed. Invariant tested: total debt created must equal total units taken, and consumed must accurately reflect this. Scoped impact: accounting desync — maker's maxAssets cap is consumed faster than debt is created. Proof idea: assert sum(units_i) == total_debt and sum(mulDivUp(units_i, sellerPrice, WAD)) == consumed[maker][group].

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the interaction between wExp's unchecked arithmetic and the negative-input branch (return 1e36 / wExp(-x)) to cause tickToPrice to return a price that is off by more than PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP for ticks near MAX_TICK/2 (the midpoint where the sigmoid is steepest), leading to consumed tracking errors that accumulate across many partial fills? Preconditions: tick near MAX_TICK/2 = 2910 where wExp argument is near 0. Call sequence: (1) compute tickToPrice(2910) and compare to true sigmoid value; (2) fill buy offer at tick 2910 with maxAssets > 0; (3) assert consumed delta accuracy. Invariant tested: tickToPrice error must be bounded by PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP for all ticks. Scoped impact: offer overfill or undercharge — price error propagates to consumed accounting. Proof idea: differential test tickToPrice(2910) against high-precision computation, assert |error| < PRICE_ROUNDING_STEP.

[File: 'src/libraries/TickLib.sol -> Scope: High Incorrect consumed-offer accounting allowing offers to be overfilled, reused, undercharged, or griefed after partial fill'] [Function: TickLib.tickToPrice / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, for a buy offer with maxAssets > 0, exploit the fact that the consumed counter is keyed by (maker, group) not by (maker, group, market), so that filling the same offer in different markets (if the maker has offers with the same group across markets) causes the consumed counter to advance faster than any single offer's maxAssets, potentially blocking fills in other markets before the maker intended? Preconditions: maker has offers in two different markets with the same group and same maxAssets. Call sequence: (1) fill offer in market A partially; (2) attempt to fill offer in market B; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] reflects fills from both markets. Invariant tested: group budget must correctly aggregate consumption across all markets sharing the group. Scoped impact: offer griefed — fills in one market exhaust the budget for offers in other markets. Proof idea: create two markets, same maker group, fill one to near maxAssets, assert second market offer is blocked.
